topic: A person discovers their mentor has retired without saying goodbye
raw_output:
[story 1]

The office felt cavernous, the silence amplified by the hum of the outdated ventilation system. Elias stood by the empty mahogany desk, the scent of aged paper and stale coffee suddenly suffocating. The framed photograph of Dr. Alistair, beaming with his signature knowing smile, seemed to mock him. Elias’s hand trembled as he picked up the final, unsent email draft—a note intended to congratulate Alistair on his well-deserved retirement—and then stopped. The silence stretched, thick and absolute. He reread the last line: "Wishing you all the best, Alistair." No reply. No indication of departure. A cold dread began to coil in his stomach, tightening until he felt breathless. He slowly lowered the paper, his vision blurring at the edges. He needed to move, to call, but his feet were rooted to the linoleum floor, paralyzed by the sheer, inexplicable void Alistair had left behind.

[story 2]

Sarah stared at the empty chair across from her, the ergonomic leather cool beneath her fingertips. The news had landed like a physical blow: Professor Davies was gone. Not just gone, but vanished from the academic landscape without a single farewell note, a single courtesy call. Sarah pressed her palms flat against her eyes, squeezing her temples until a sharp pain flared behind her brow. Her breath hitched, shallow and quick. She walked to the window, looking out at the indifferent city traffic, feeling a profound imbalance in her chest, as if something vital had been ripped away. "No," she whispered, the sound thin and reedy in the quiet room. She turned back, her gaze sweeping over the meticulously organized bookshelves, every volume suddenly seeming accusatory. The air in the room felt too thin, insufficient for the weight of the absence.

[story 3]

Mark found the notice tucked beneath Alistair’s usual reading chair. It was a formal announcement, stark and impersonal, detailing the cessation of all professional duties. There was no personal touch, no mention of colleagues, no acknowledgement of the years shared in shared struggle and late-night breakthroughs. Mark read the typed words three times, his jaw clenching so tightly he could feel the strain in his molars. A wave of nausea washed over him, making the fluorescent lights overhead flicker erratically. He stood motionless, the paper crinkling sound deafening in the quiet study. His hands began to shake uncontrollably, and he had to press them hard against his thighs to steady himself. The world seemed to tilt slightly on its axis, leaving him stranded in a sudden, sickening vertigo.
